coredata.dc:title: Germanium nanoparticles: Intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity and its biosensing application
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jiali
Lu, Qiujun
Wu, Cuiyan
Liu, Meiling
Li, Haitao
Zhang, Youyu
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, germanium nanoparticles (Ge NPs) have attracted considerable research attention because of its excellent electrical and optical properties. However, it is mainly applied in the field of solar cells, photodetectors and photothermal therapy. Actually, Ge NPs have the advantages of low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, which means that they have great potential in biosensing applications. However, there are few related reports. In this paper, we discovered that Ge NPs have the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity and can significantly accelerate the oxidation of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 for the first time. Compared with natural enzyme (horseradish peroxidase, HRP), Ge NPs maintain higher catalytic stability after treatment at different pH, temperature and ionic strength. Furthermore, a Ge NPs-based colorimetric platform was established for the first time to detect biomolecules with favourable sensitivity and selectivity, such as uric acid, glucose and choline. This work finds the new property of Ge NPs and extends its application in biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Germanium nanoparticles
Peroxidase-like activity
Kinetics study
Colorimetric platform
Biomolecules detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dependence of pH, catalyst concentration, and H2O2
Robustness analysis of the Ge NPs
Ge NPs-based colorimetric platform for biomolecules detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of as-synthesized Ge NPs; inset: the HRTEM image of single Ge NPs. (B) Size distribution histograms of as-synthesized Ge NPs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of as-synthesized Ge NPs. (D) UV–vis and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of as-synthesized Ge NPs.

The effects of Ge NPs in various reaction systems. (a) TMB, Ge NPs and H2O2, (b) TMB, and H2O2, (c) Ge NPs and TMB. Reaction conditions: the solutions were incubated in 0.05 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.0) at 37 °C for 30 min. Inset: the color changes of the different samples.

The effect of the concentration of Ge NPs on the formation of hydroxyl radical with terephthalic acid as a fluorescence probe. (a) 50 mg Ge NPs without H2O2; (b-j) 0, 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90, 105, 120 μg mL−1 and 2 mM H2O2. Reaction conditions: 0.5 mM terephthalic acid and different solutions were incubated in 0.05 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.0) for 3 h.

Steady-state kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism of Ge NPs (A-D). The velocity (v) of the reaction was measured using 75 μL Ge NPs in 0.05 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.0) at 37 °C. (A) The concentration of TMB was 1 mM and H2O2 concentration was varied. (B) The concentration of H2O2 was 0.5 mM and TMB concentration was varied. (C, D) Double reciprocal plots of activity of Ge NPs with the concentration of one substrate (TMB or H2O2) fixed and the other varied.

(A) The absorption spectra of the colorimetric sensor in the presence of different concentrations of UA. (B) A doseresponse curves for UA detection in the range of 2.5–500 μM using Ge NPs as a peroxidase mimic. Error bars show the standard deviation from three parallel measurements. Inset: Image of colored products in the presence of different concentrations of UA (From left to right: 0, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 300, 500 μM).

Selectivity of colorimetric sensor for the detection of UA (o: 0.15 mM). The possible interferences are serine (a), lysine (b), glycine (c), histidine (d), proline (e), ascorbic acid (f), cysteine (g), dopamine (h), glutathione (i), glucose (j), urea (k), K+ (l), Na+ (m), cytosine (n). The concentration of glutathione was 1.5 mM, and the concentration of other potential interfering substances was 2.5 mM.
The intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Ge NPs and its biosensing application.
Recoveries of the determination of uric acid in human serum sample.

head.ce:title: Germanium nanoparticles: Intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity and its biosensing application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, germanium nanoparticles (Ge NPs) have attracted considerable research attention because of its excellent electrical and optical properties. However, it is mainly applied in the field of solar cells, photodetectors and photothermal therapy. Actually, Ge NPs have the advantages of low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, which means that they have great potential in biosensing applications. However, there are few related reports. In this paper, we discovered that Ge NPs have the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity and can significantly accelerate the oxidation of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 for the first time. Compared with natural enzyme (horseradish peroxidase, HRP), Ge NPs maintain higher catalytic stability after treatment at different pH, temperature and ionic strength. Furthermore, a Ge NPs-based colorimetric platform was established for the first time to detect biomolecules with favourable sensitivity and selectivity, such as uric acid, glucose and choline. This work finds the new property of Ge NPs and extends its application in biosensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Group IV (C, Si and Ge) nanoparticles (NPs) are the well-studied nanomaterials with tremendous application prospect in many fields, due to their attractive advantages, such as chemical stability, prominent compatibility and relatively low toxicity [1–5]. In recent years, C and Si NPs have received widespread attention in biosensing, such as detect nucleotides [6], metal ions [7–9], drugs [10], some small molecules [11–14], and so on, due to the merits of low cost, easy synthesis, favourable hydrophilcity and excellent surface tailorability. Ge has larger excitonic Bohr radius than that of C and Si [15,16], implying more remarkable quantum confinement effects and easier changes in the electronic structure around the bandgap [17,18], so that Ge nanomaterials have enormous potential for practical application. Recently, Ge nanomaterials have been successfully applied in some fields, including lithium-ion batteries [19,20] and optoelectronic devices [21]. For instance, Ge possesses high theoretical capacity (1600 mA h g−1 for Li4.4Ge), fast lithium-ion diffusion coefficient (about 400 times higher than Si) and high electrical conductivity (about 100 times better than Si) [22,23], so Ge nanomaterials have been expected to own better electrochemical performance and considered as the promising anode materials for lithium-ion batteries [19,24,25]. In addition, because of the lower preparation temperature and larger optical absorption efficiency, Ge and Ge-based nanomaterials have significant application in optoelectronic devices, including photodetectors, solar cells and field-effect transistors [21,26,27]. Besides, Ge nanomaterials have low toxicity [28], size-dependent optical properties [29] and so on, implying they have great potential for biosensing. However, there are few reports about the application of Ge NPs in biosensors, probably because of some shortcomings of the synthetic methods. For instance, some strategies require rigorous conditions or high temperature, and the relatively poor dispersity or hydrophilcity of the synthesized Ge NPs [30,31]. Recently, our research group developed a new method to obtain water dispersed and hydrophilous Ge NPs [32]. Therefore, exploring unknown merits and developing new utilization of Ge NPs are extremely important to extend the application of Ge NPs.

Enzymes are of great significance in the fields of agricultural production, biosensing, clinical diagnosis and environmental protection [33–36]. But they bear some limitations, such as complicated preparation and purification procedure, sensitivity to environmental changes and inherent instability [37–40]. In recent years, nanozymes have brought about increasing attentions because of their large specific surface areas, low cost, easy preparation, and high stability under various conditions [41–44]. In this work, we testified that Ge NPs have an intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity for the first time. Analysis of the enzyme catalytic kinetics, stability in different environmental factors and peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism of Ge NPs were also explored. Furthermore, based on the catalytic property of Ge NPs, combined with other oxidase, a sensitive and selective colorimetric platform was constructed for the detection of biomolecules, which is shown in Scheme 1. This is also the first time that Ge NPs have been used for biosensing by colorimetry. Taking uric acid (UA) as an example, this sensing platform was successfully used for uric acid determination in serum with satisfactory results.

Tetrabutoxygermane (97%) was purchased from energy-chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was obtained from shanghai Regal Co., Ltd·H2O2 (30 wt%), uric acid, serine, lysine, glycine, histidine, proline, ascorbic acid, cysteine, dopamine, glutathione, glucose and urea were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent. Uricase, glucose oxidase and choline oxidase were purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human serum sample was afforded by the hospital of Hunan Normal University (Changsha, China). Other regents of analytical grade were obtained from Beijing Chemical Company (Beijing, China) and were used as received without any further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1; Millpore., USA) was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were captured on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained by using a Nicolet Nexus 670 FT-IR spectroscope (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA) in the 4000–400 cm−1 regions. UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were measured by using an UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Japan) and a QM40 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PTI Ltd, Canada).

Ge NPs were prepared by one-step aqueous-solution method [32]. Briefly, 0.1 mL tetrabutoxygermane and 0.016 g NaBH4 were added to 6 mL aqueous solution while stirring. Then, the total mixture was put into an oil bath at 80 °C for 12 h. At last, the Ge NPs were formed and then dialyzed (Mw 500) against water for two days.

The peroxidase-like activity was investigated by the reaction mixtures containing H2O2 and chromogenic substrates TMB in the presence of Ge NPs. In a typical experiment, 100 μL of the Ge NPs solution (75 μg mL−1) was added into 850 μL NaAc buffer solution (50 mM, pH 4.0), followed by adding 10 μL of 50 mM TMB solution and 40 μL of H2O2 solution with different concentrations, then kept in 37 °C for 30 min before absorption measurement.

The Kinetic measurements were carried out in 1 mL NaAc buffer solution (50 mM, pH 4.0) containing 75 μg mL−1 Ge NPs and different concentrations of H2O2 or TMB. The Michaelis-Menten constants (Km) were calculated by using Lineweaver-Burk plots of the double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten equation, 1/ν = Km/Vmax (1/[S] + 1/Km), where ν is the rate of conversion, Vmax is the maximum rate of conversion and [S] is the substrate concentration.

The hydroxyl radicals were detected by a fluorometric method using terephthalic acid (TA) as a probe. Briefly, H2O2 (2 mM), TA (0.5 mM) and the Ge NPs with different concentrations were incubated in NaAc buffer solution (50 mM, pH 4.0) at 37 °C for 3 h. After the reaction period, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 315 nm.

The calibration curve of the detection of UA was carried out as follows: a) 50 μL of 5 mg mL−1 UOx and 50 μL of UA with different concentrations were incubated at 37 °C for 40 min; b) the obtained solution were mixed with 10 μL of 50 mM TMB, 100 μL of Ge NPs (100 mg mL−1), and 790 μL of NaAc buffer solution (50 mM, pH 4.0) for another 30 min at 37 °C; c) the resulting solution was used for calibration curve measurement.

For UA detection in serum, the serum sample was first treated by ultra filtration with 5 kDa in the amicon cell at 3000 rpm for 30 min. Before subsequent use, the serum sample was diluted 40 times. Then, the subsequent procedure for UA analysis was the same as above.

The Ge NPs were successfully prepared by adopting tetrabutoxygermane as the precursor and NaBH4 as the reducing agent. The morphology, structural characterization and optical properties of as-synthesized Ge NPs were investigated. Fig. 1A shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the as-synthesized Ge NPs, which are spherical in shape and have high crystallinity. The average size is 2.4 ± 0.4 nm in diameter, showed in Fig. 1B. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1C) demonstrates that the peaks at 1450 cm−1 and 1060 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibration of Ge-C bonds and Ge-O-C bonds respectively. In addition, the stretching vibration of Ge-O bonds at 800–900 cm−1 illustrates the surface of the Ge NPs are partially oxidated [45]. The UV–vis absorption (red) and fluorescence properties (black) of the as-synthesized Ge NPs are displayed in Fig. 1D. The Ge NPs not only have a strong absorbance in the UV region, but also display bright green fluorescence with the maximum emission at the wavelength of 505 nm. Besides, by calculation, we concluded that its fluorescence quantum yield is 5.6% (using quinine sulfate as reference).

We testified the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the Ge NPs by using representative TMB-H2O2 reaction, which can be demonstrated by the formation of blue TMB oxidation product. Fig. 2 manifests the UV–vis absorption spectra of this experiment. In the absence of Ge NPs (curve b), the characteristic absorption peaks of oxidized TMB (oxTMB) at 370 nm and 652 nm were weak. When in the presence of Ge NPs, the absorption peaks at 370 nm and 652 nm increased intensively (curve a), demonstrating that Ge NPs expedited the TMB-H2O2 reaction markedly. In addition, no absorption at 370 nm and 652 nm were observed in the case of Ge NPs and TMB (curve c), indicating the Ge NPs could not oxidize TMB without H2O2. Accordingly, as seen the insets in Fig. 2, we can distinctly find out that a conspicuous blue change also appeared in the presence of the Ge NPs in the TMB-H2O2 system. To further verify the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the Ge NPs, we used other peroxidase substrates to take place of TMB, such as 2,2-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). As shown in Fig. S1, similar to the catalytic effect of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), we observed green product and yellow product respectively. All of the observations affirmed that the Ge NPs possess an intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity, which is similar to HRP (Eq. (1)).
(1)
H
2
O
2
+ T M B
⟶
G e N P s

2
O + o x T M B

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dependence of pH, catalyst concentration, and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to HRP and other nanomaterial-based peroxidase mimetics, the catalytic activity of Ge NPs also relies on pH, catalyst concentration, and H2O2 concentration. In Fig. S2, the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Ge NPs was investigated by varying the pH from 3.0 to 5.5, the catalyst concentration from 0 to 125 μg mL−1, and the H2O2 concentration from 0.005 to 1.0 mM. As shown in Fig. S2A, the absorbance intensity at 652 nm goes up with pH in the range of 3.0–4.0, and then decreases with the increasing pH, indicating the optimal pH was 4.0. This may be due to the decomposition of H2O2 at higher pH [46], leading to a decrease in the oxidation ability of H2O2 to TMB. Fig. S2B indicates the relationship between the speed of colorimetric reaction and Ge NPs concentration, and a linear relationship is acquired at the range of 0–75 μg mL−1 Ge NPs. So we chose 75 μg mL−1 Ge NPs as the catalyst concentration of following experiments. Analogously, the effect of H2O2 concentration was shown in Fig. S2C, which revealed the speed of colorimetric reaction is proportional to the H2O2 concentration in the range of 1–100 μM, explaining that the strategy can be used to sensitive detection of trace H2O2. Meanwhile, the catalytic ability of Ge NPs was not inhibited at high H2O2 concentration, which is contrary to the HRP enzyme [47], indicating that the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Ge NPs is more stable at high H2O2 concentrations.

The peroxidase-like catalytic activity mechanism of Ge NPs was investigated by using terephthalic acid (TA) as a fluorescence probe. The effect of Ge NPs on the signal intensity of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) can be evaluated because TA can capture ·OH to generate highly fluorescent product [48]. It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity increased with the amount of Ge NPs, indicating that the amount of generated ·OH were enhanced by the increase in Ge NPs ( Fig. 3). However, we observed no fluorescence in the absence of H2O2. These results make it clear that Ge NPs can expedite the decomposition of H2O2 to form ·OH.

Amperometric responses of the Ge NPs modified glassy carbon electrode (Ge NPs/GCE) were further researched to prove its catalytic mechanism. As shown in Fig. S3, compared with GCE, the current response of Ge NPs/GCE significantly increases in the solution containing H2O2 and TMB. The result clearly testified that Ge NPs can enhance the electrocatalytic activity in the reaction of H2O2 and TMB. The activity was attributed to the acceleration of electron transfer between H2O2 and TMB.

To better understand the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Ge NPs, the steady-state kinetic data were acquired by changing one substrate concentration while keeping the other substrate concentration invariable. As shown in Figs. 4A and 4B, the typical Michaelis-Menten curves were found for the Ge NPs within a certain concentration of H2O2 or TMB. We calculated the Michaelis constant (Km) using the Lineweaver-Burk plots (Figs. 4C and 4D), manifesting the binding affinity of Ge NPs to H2O2 or TMB. The results were summarized in Table S1. The Km of Ge NPs toward H2O2 is 0.187 mM, which is less than that of HRP (3.70 mM), demonstrating that Ge NPs possess a higher affinity toward H2O2 [49]. The Km of Ge NPs toward TMB is similar to that of HRP (0.434 mM), suggesting that the Ge NPs exhibit similar affinity for TMB compared with HRP [49]. Moreover, in the Lineaweaver-Burk plots (Figs. 4C and 4D), the lines are nearly parallel, which is in line with the Ping-pong mechanism. In other words, the Ge NPs bind to the first substrate (H2O2) and discharge of the first product before interacting with the second substrate (TMB), which is similar to HRP [50] and some nanomaterials [37]. As TA fluorescence experiment had pointed out, this intermediate should be ·OH which originated from the catalytic decomposition of H2O2 by Ge NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Robustness analysis of the Ge NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of catalyst under rigorous conditions is of vital importance to expand their application prospects; however, the activity of natural enzymes is susceptible to environmental factors. We conjecture that Ge NPs are more stable than natural enzymes. Thus, the robustness of Ge NPs and HRP were investigated. For this, before carrying out the peroxidase-like catalytic activity experiment, the Ge NPs and HRP were incubated at the wide range of pH (1.0–14.0), temperatures (10–90 °C) and different NaCl concentrations (0–2.0 M) for 1 h. Results in Fig. S4 indicate that Ge NPs maintain most of the Peroxidase-like catalytic activity (~90%) after treatment at different pH, temperature and NaCl concentrations. In contrast, the enzyme activity of HRP would markedly decline at pH ﹤ 3, pH ﹥ 11 or at temperature ﹥ 50 °C under the similar operations. Moreover, the catalytic activity of HRP is significantly reduced as the salt concentration increases. Hence, it is obvious that the Ge NPs are more stable than natural HRP and exhibit more excellent potential for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ge NPs-based colorimetric platform for biomolecules detection
ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 can be obtained from the oxidases-catalysed oxidation of their specific substrate. Combined with the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Ge NPs, a colorimetric platform has been built to detect biomolecules by measuring the absorbance intensity of the oxidized TMB (at 652 nm) (Scheme 1). As an example, uricase (UOx) was cooperated with Ge NPs to form a colorimetric sensor to detect UA. As shown in Fig. 5A, the absorption peaks at 652 nm increase with UA concentration ranging from 0 to 0.5 mM. The linear range is 2.5–150 μM (R2 = 0.9932) with a detection limit of 1.1 μM (Fig. 5B). A clear color change has appeared with the increase in UA concentration (see Fig. 3B inset), indicating this sensor provided a simple and sensitive way for UA detection. Aside from UA, sensor for detection of other substances was also viable. Fig. S5 and Fig. S6 show that when Ge NPs combined with glucose oxidase or choline oxidase, the absorption peak at 652 nm is proportional to the concentration of glucose or choline, manifesting the universality of this strategy.

To evaluate the selectivity for UA detection in human serum, the control experiments were carried out by using some possible interfering substances, including serine, lysine, glycine, histidine, proline, ascorbic acid, cysteine, dopamine, glutathione, glucose, urea, K+, Na+, cytosine. As shown in Fig. 6, even when the concentrations of interfering substances are 10 times larger than UA, the absorbance of UA is considerably higher than that of interfering substances. It is evident that this colorimetric sensor provides high selectivity for UA detection.

The determination of uric acid in blood serum is very significant for the diagnosis of related diseases [51–54]. Hence, the feasibility of this colorimetric sensor was studied for the detection UA concentration in human serum. As shown in Table 1, according to the standard curve, the measured results of UA concentration is 289.68 μM in serum sample. The accuracy of this colorimetric sensor was explored by adding UA with different concentrations (20, 50 and 80 μM). The recoveries of UA are between 93.25% and 101.21%, with the RSD ranging from 2.6% to 3.4%. This colorimetric sensor offers a reliable and precise method for the practical detection of UA.

In conclusion, we first presented the report that the Ge NPs have intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity and exhibit excellent catalysis in resisting pH and temperature change than natural enzymes. The steady-state kinetic analysis showed that the Ge NPs possess higher affinity to H2O2 than HRP and the catalytic behavior of Ge NPs conform to Ping-pang mechanism. The catalytic mechanism we identified is that Ge NPs can promote the electron transfer between TMB and H2O2 and catalyze the production of ·OH by H2O2. Furthermore, the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Ge NPs provided a sensitive, selective and reliable platform for the colorimetric sensing of biomolecules. It is undoubted that our research not only discovered the new property of Ge NPs, but also broadened the potential application for Ge NPs in biotechnology and clinical diagnostics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475043, 21874042, 21675051, 21375037) and Foundation of the Science & Technology Department of Hunan Province (2016SK2020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.11.081.
